SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates PositionRank on multiple scholarly-paper datasets (KDD, WWW, Nguyen & Kan) and compares against several strong baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau, Wan & Xiao, Hulth, Hasan & Ng) using standard metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1), which provides reasonable empirical support for the main claims. However, there are important reproducibility and analysis gaps: hyperparameter settings are not reported, code/data release is not mentioned, and no ablation or sensitivity analyses are provided to isolate the contribution of the position-bias, window size, POS filtering, or phrase-scoring choices. Related work coverage is reportedly adequate, but the paper fails to discuss limitations or failure cases. Given solid comparative experiments but missing ablations and reproducibility details, the evaluation is adequate but incomplete. | B(3): The experiments compare PositionRank to multiple strong baselines across three scholarly-paper datasets and report sizable gains, which provides reasonable empirical support for the main claim. However, the paper lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of the position bias, window size, candidate filtering), does not report hyperparameters or code for reproducibility, and omits discussion of limitations or failure cases. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the missing methodological detail and analyses prevent a fully convincing validation of the approach.

However, there are important reproducibility and analysis gaps: hyperparameter settings are not reported, code/data release is not mentioned, and no ablation or sensitivity analyses are provided to isolate the contribution of the position-bias, window size, POS filtering, or phrase-scoring choices. Related work coverage is reportedly adequate, but the paper fails to discuss limitations or failure cases. Given solid comparative experiments but missing ablations and reproducibility details, the evaluation is adequate but incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments compare PositionRank to multiple strong baselines across three scholarly-paper datasets and report sizable gains, which provides reasonable empirical support for the main claim. However, the paper lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of the position bias, window size, candidate filtering), does not report hyperparameters or code for reproducibility, and omits discussion of limitations or failure cases. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the missing methodological detail and analyses prevent a fully convincing validation of the approach.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses an important practical problem—keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents—and shows sizable empirical gains (up to 29.09%) over strong, established baselines by introducing a position-biased PageRank and candidate construction tailored to nouns/adjectives. From my reproducibility-focused perspective, the lack of reported hyperparameters and unspecified code release reduces the immediate practical uptake and re-use by researchers and practitioners. The idea of incorporating positional signals into graph-based ranking is useful and likely to be built upon, but it appears incremental rather than transformative across NLP beyond this subfield. | B(3): The paper addresses a useful problem—unsupervised keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents—and proposes a clear, practically-motivated modification (position-biased PageRank) that yields substantial gains (up to 29.09%) over standard PageRank and several established baselines on multiple research-paper datasets (KDD, WWW, Nguyen & Kan). From my perspective as an NLP/IR researcher, these results make a compelling case that position information is important and will influence how practitioners design unsupervised extractors, and the method is likely to be reused or extended. However, the contribution is an incremental, conceptually straightforward enhancement to existing graph-based extractors rather than a paradigm shift, so its impact is moderate rather than transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The topic—unsupervised keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents—clearly lies within the NLP/IR remit of top NLP/ML conferences and the paper compares against strong, relevant baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau, Hulth, Wan & Xiao, Hasan & Ng) on established datasets (KDD, WWW, Nguyen & Kan). Methods and evaluation (position-biased PageRank, MRR/precision/recall/F1) meet community expectations. However, important reproducibility details are missing in the summary (no code release, hyperparameter settings not reported, and no stated limitations), which are significant for top-tier acceptance and reduce confidence in claimed improvements despite solid baselines and evaluation. | B(4): This paper lies squarely within top-tier NLP/IR conference scope: unsupervised keyphrase extraction is a core NLP task and the proposed position-biased PageRank is a clear methodological contribution. The datasets (KDD, WWW, Nguyen and Kan) and strong baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau; Wan & Xiao; Hulth; Hasan & Ng) and standard metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1) match community expectations. Minor concerns for a top venue are absence of reported hyperparameters, no mention of code release, and no discussion of limitations, but these do not undermine overall audience fit.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares PositionRank against a strong set of established baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004, Wan & Xiao 2008, Hulth 2003, Hasan & Ng 2014 and PageRank variants) on standard scholarly-paper datasets (KDD, WWW, Nguyen & Kan) using appropriate metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1). From a reproducibility and fairness perspective the comparisons appear to use the same data and evaluation metrics, but the summary notes missing details such as hyperparameter reporting and code release, and it does not mention whether more recent neural or supervised baselines were considered. The manuscript explains the methodological differences (position-biased PageRank and candidate formation) and reports sizable improvements, but lacks explicit discussion of cases where prior methods might perform comparably and omits some reproducibility artifacts, so I mark it as strong but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper evaluates PositionRank against established and relevant unsupervised and supervised baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004, Wan & Xiao 2008, Hulth 2003, Hasan & Ng 2014) on standard scholarly-paper datasets (KDD, WWW, Nguyen & Kan 2007) using common metrics (MRR, precision, recall, F1), which indicates generally fair and appropriate comparisons. However, the summary notes no reporting of hyperparameters or code availability and no stated limitations, and it’s unclear whether more recent neural/supervised ranking approaches were considered or whether the authors discuss cases where prior methods match or exceed their performance—hence a minor omission prevents a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares PositionRank against a strong set of established baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004, Wan & Xiao 2008, Hulth 2003, Hasan & Ng 2014 and PageRank variants) on standard scholarly-paper datasets (KDD, WWW, Nguyen & Kan) using appropriate metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1). From a reproducibility and fairness perspective the comparisons appear to use the same data and evaluation metrics, but the summary notes missing details such as hyperparameter reporting and code release, and it does not mention whether more recent neural or supervised baselines were considered. The manuscript explains the methodological differences (position-biased PageRank and candidate formation) and reports sizable improvements, but lacks explicit discussion of cases where prior methods might perform comparably and omits some reproducibility artifacts, so I mark it as strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates PositionRank against established and relevant unsupervised and supervised baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004, Wan & Xiao 2008, Hulth 2003, Hasan & Ng 2014) on standard scholarly-paper datasets (KDD, WWW, Nguyen & Kan 2007) using common metrics (MRR, precision, recall, F1), which indicates generally fair and appropriate comparisons. However, the summary notes no reporting of hyperparameters or code availability and no stated limitations, and it’s unclear whether more recent neural/supervised ranking approaches were considered or whether the authors discuss cases where prior methods match or exceed their performance—hence a minor omission prevents a top score.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): PositionRank presents a notable and clearly differentiated extension of graph-based keyphrase extraction by introducing a position-biased PageRank that aggregates information from all occurrence positions—an idea not present in the cited PageRank-based baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau, Wan & Xiao). The paper applies this bias within the standard co-occurrence noun/adjective graph and candidate-phrase composition, so while the problem and basic pipeline are well-studied, the proposed positional bias is a non-trivial, targeted modification that would likely inform experts and yield measurable gains (as reported on multiple scholarly-paper datasets). Given the summary, the contribution is more than incremental combination but stops short of a fundamentally new problem formulation, so I rate it as notable novelty rather than groundbreaking. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and clear methodological novelty by introducing a position-biased PageRank that aggregates information from all occurrence positions of words—an explicit, non-trivial extension of graph-based keyphrase extraction methods like TextRank/PageRank. The work distinguishes itself from prior PageRank-based and statistical baselines (Mihalcea and Tarau 2004; Wan and Xiao 2008; Hulth 2003; Hasan and Ng 2014) and demonstrates substantial empirical gains on multiple scholarly-paper datasets, so it would inform experts while not radically redefining the problem formulation itself.

The work distinguishes itself from prior PageRank-based and statistical baselines (Mihalcea and Tarau 2004; Wan and Xiao 2008; Hulth 2003; Hasan and Ng 2014) and demonstrates substantial empirical gains on multiple scholarly-paper datasets, so it would inform experts while not radically redefining the problem formulation itself.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear empirical claims and compares PositionRank against several strong baselines on multiple scholarly-paper datasets, reporting sizable improvements, which supports the core claim. However, the summary indicates no release of code, no reporting of hyperparameters, and no mention of statistical significance or variance estimates, which undermines reproducibility and the strength of experimental evidence. The mathematical component (biased PageRank) appears conceptually sound from the summary but no detailed derivations or proofs are noted. Overall the methodology is promising and partially validated, but missing experimental controls and reproducibility artifacts justify a moderate score. | B(4): The paper's main claim—that incorporating information from all occurrence positions into a biased PageRank (PositionRank) improves keyphrase extraction—is clearly stated and supported by experimental comparisons showing up to 29% gains on multiple scholarly-paper datasets against reasonable baselines (including prior PageRank-based methods). Mathematical content is lightweight (graph construction and biased PageRank) and appears correct; no complex proofs were required. However, experimental methodology has shortcomings: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and the summary does not indicate statistical significance testing or ablations, which weakens reproducibility and confidence in some claims. Overall the conclusions are plausible and largely supported, but minor methodological omissions prevent a technically flawless assessment.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from problem to method to experiments, and the main contributions (PositionRank and its use of occurrence positions) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the writing quality is good; figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and informative. Minor presentation issues include the absence of hyperparameter reporting and no stated limitations, which reduce clarity for readers attempting to reproduce results. Overall the exposition is strong but could better surface reproducibility-relevant details. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions are stated early and repeatedly (PositionRank and position-biased PageRank). Notation and methodological descriptions appear clear for the construction of the word graph and phrase scoring, though lack of reported hyperparameters and no mentioned code reduce reproducibility clarity. Figures/tables are not described in the summary so I assume they are adequate; writing quality is good with related work coverage adequate, leaving only minor presentation issues (e.g., missing hyperparameter details and explicit limitations).

main_claims: Proposes PositionRank, an unsupervised model for keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents that incorporates information from all positions of a word’s occurrences into a biased PageRank.
PositionRank outperforms PageRank models that do not account for word positions as well as other strong baselines for keyphrase extraction.
On several datasets of research papers, PositionRank achieves improvements up to 29.09% over compared methods.
_summary.methodology: Builds an undirected word graph from nouns and adjectives that co-occur within a window; applies a position-biased PageRank to score words using information from all occurrence positions; forms candidate phrases matching (adjective)*(noun)+ up to trigrams and scores phrases by summing their constituent word scores.
datasets: Nguyen and Kan (2007) dataset (research papers from various disciplines)
baselines: PageRank models that do not take into account word positions

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating NLP pipelines for information extraction and IR systems in industry and academia. Experience includes implementing graph-based ranking algorithms, large-scale evaluations, and producing reproducible benchmarks.
traits: insists on clear experimental protocols, hyperparameter settings, and code/data release to reproduce results
looks for strong and correctly implemented baselines, as well as appropriate evaluation metrics and statistical significance
values ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each component (e.g., position bias, window size, POS filtering)
checks robustness to preprocessing choices and sensitivity to hyperparameters (e.g., candidate selection, graph construction)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments and implementation support the claimed improvements, requesting missing details, controlled comparisons, and reproducibility artifacts.

reviewer_b.name: Research Impact Reviewer
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in NLP and IR with a track record of work on keyphrase extraction, document representation, and scholarly search; regularly reviews for top NLP/IR venues and edits special issues on extraction methods.
traits: evaluates novelty with respect to prior graph-based and position-aware keyphrase methods and prioritizes conceptual contributions
assesses practical significance for downstream applications (e.g., search, summarization, metadata generation)
notices connections and distinctions to related lines (supervised neural models, supervised ranking, and heuristic baselines) and values clear articulation of trade-offs
values clarity about dataset selection and real-world generalizability across domains and genres
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Emphasizes where the work advances the field, how it compares to prior art, and whether the methodological choices lead to meaningful practical impact and new research directions.
